1: בְּךָ יְהוָה חָסִיתִי (bĕkā YHWH ḥāsîtî, "In you, LORD, I take refuge") — ḥāsāh: to take refuge, to seek shelter (as under the wings of a parent bird, Psalms 57:1). The perfect tense of settled trust: "I have taken refuge and remain there." בְּצִדְקָתְךָ פַלְּטֵנִי (bĕṣidqātĕkā pallĕṭênî, "rescue me by your righteousness") — ṣĕdāqāh: righteousness, covenant-faithfulness. The rescue is grounded in the LORD's own covenantal integrity — not in the Psalmist's merit. God's righteousness means he must rescue those who trust in him, because his covenant commits him to it.
2: הַטֵּה אֵלַי אָזְנְךָ מְהֵרָה הַצִּילֵנִי (haṭṭeh ʾêlay ʾāznĕkā mĕhêrāh haṣṣîlênî, "Turn your ear to me; quickly rescue me") — nāṭāh (hiphil: to incline/bend down to hear a faint voice). Mĕhêrāh: quickly, speedily — urgency. לְצוּר-מָעוֹז לְבֵית מְצוּדוֹת לְהוֹשִׁיעֵנִי — the cluster of spatial metaphors: ṣûr (rock/cliff), māʿôz (stronghold), bêt mĕṣûdôt (house of fortresses) — layered protection: rock → fortress → fortified building within the fortress.
3: כִּי-סַלְעִי וּמְצוּדָתִי אָתָּה (kî-salʿî ûmĕṣûdātî ʾāttāh, "For you are my rock and my fortress") — selaʿ: the cliff-face of rock (as in Psalms 18:2). וּלְמַעַן שִׁמְךָ תַּנְחֵנִי וּתְנַהֲלֵנִי (ûlĕmaʿan shimkā tanḥênî ûtĕnahălênî, "lead me and guide me for your name's sake") — nāḥāh: to guide (as a shepherd leads); nāhal: to lead gently to water (as a shepherd leads to watering-places, Psalms 23:2). The double shepherding verbs. Lĕmaʿan shimkā: "for the sake of your name" — God's own reputation as the motivation for his action.
4: תּוֹצִיאֵנִי מֵרֶשֶׁת זוּ טָמְנוּ-לִי (tôṣîʾênî mêreshet zû ṭāmĕnû-lî, "Free me from the trap they have secretly set for me") — reshet: a net, a web — the hunting net laid for birds. Ṭāman: to hide, to conceal — the trap is secretly laid; the danger is not visible until it springs. The enemies as hunters who hide their devices.
5: בְּיָדְךָ אַפְקִיד רוּחִי (bĕyādĕkā ʾapqîd rûḥî, "Into your hand I commit my spirit") — pāqad (hiphil: to entrust, to deposit for safekeeping — the legal term for depositing valuables into another's custody). Rûaḥ: spirit, breath — the animating life-force, the innermost self. Jesus quotes this exact verse from the cross in Luke 23:46 (LXX: εἰς χεῖράς σου παρατίθεμαι τὸ πνεῦμά μου), making his death the ultimate act of trust in the Father. In Jewish evening prayer this verse was used daily — the surrender of the breath-self to divine keeping through the night. פָּדִיתָה אוֹתִי יְהוָה אֵל אֱמֶת (pādîtāh ʾôtî YHWH ʾêl ʾĕmet, "you have redeemed me, LORD, faithful God") — pādāh: to ransom, to redeem.
6: שָׂנֵאתִי הַשֹּׁמְרִים הַבְלֵי-שָׁוְא (śānêtî hashomĕrîm habalê-shāwľ, "I despise those who cling to worthless idols") — shamar (to keep, to cling to — here used for idol-devotion): the same verb for keeping commandments, deployed negatively for idol-adherence. Habalê-shāwľ: "vanities of emptiness" — hebel (breath/vanity — the word of Ecclesiastes) combined with shāwľ (worthlessness). The double-emptiness of idols. וַאֲנִי אֶל-יְהוָה בָּטָחְתִּי — the contrast: idol-clinging vs bāṭaḥ (trusting, leaning-on) that connects to the real.
7: אָגִילָה וְאֶשְׂמְחָה בְּחַסְדֶּךָ (ʾāgîlāh wĕʾesmĕḥāh bĕḥasdekā, "I will be glad and rejoice in your steadfast love") — gîl and śāmaḥ: twin verbs of joy (same pairing as Psalms 21:1). Ḥesed: covenant-loyalty love. אֲשֶׁר רָאִיתָ אֶת-עָנְיִי יָדַעְתָּ בְּצָרוֹת נַפְשִׁי — rāʾāh: to see; yādaʿ: to know experientially. God not only observed the distress from a distance but knew it from within, entering into the experience of the Psalmist's suffering.
8: וְלֹא הִסְגַּרְתַּנִי בְּיַד-אוֹיֵב (wĕlōʾ hisgartanî bĕyad-ʾôyêb, "you did not hand me over to the enemy") — sāgar (hiphil: to deliver over as a prisoner): not handing over to the enemy. הֶעֱמַדְתָּ בַמֶּרְחָב רַגְלָי (heʿĕmadtā bammerhāb raglāy, "you set my feet in a wide-open place") — merehāb again (as in Psalms 18:19; Psalms 4:1): salvation as liberation from the tight place (ṣar) into the wide space. God sets the feet in the open space — stable footing in an unconstrained environment.
9: כִּי צָרָה-לִי עֵינִי כִּי נַפְשִׁי וּבִטְנִי (kî ṣārāh-lî ʿênî kî napshî ûbiṭnî, "my eye wastes with grief — my soul and my body too") — grief affects three registers: ʿayin (eye — what perceives the world), nāpesh (soul — the animating self), and beṭen (belly/body — the physical container). The eye that has wasted (ʿāshash: to shrink, to waste) is the eye that has wept until hollow.
10: כִּי-כָלוּ בְיָגוֹן חַיַּי (kî-kālû bĕyāgôn ḥayyay, "For my life is exhausted by grief") — kālāh: to be completed/finished — life consumed by yāgôn (grief, sorrow). כָּשַׁל בַּעֲוֹנִי כֹחִי (kāshal baʿăwōnî kōḥî, "my strength fails because of my iniquity") — kāshal: to stumble, to stagger; ʿāwôn: iniquity, guilt. The confession that moral failure has physical consequences. ʿAṣmôtay ʿāshōshû: "my bones waste away" — the skeleton as deepest structural framework, now deteriorating.
11: מִכָּל-צֹרְרַי הָיִיתִי חֶרְפָּה (mikkol-ṣōrĕray hāyîtî ḥerpāh, "Among all my enemies I am an object of scorn") — ḥerpāh: reproach, scorn, shame — the public shame that falls on the defeated and guilty. לִמְיֻדָּעַי מְאֹד — mĕyuddāʿ (those who know me): the people who knew the Psalmist in his prosperity now flee from him. Social abandonment accompanies disgrace — a condition that mirrors Jesus' abandonment at the cross (Luke 23:49: "those who knew him stood at a distance").
12: נִשְׁכַּחְתִּי כְּמֵת מִלֵּב (nishkaḥtî kĕmêt millêb, "I am as forgotten as a dead man") — shākaḥ (niphal: to be forgotten): the social death of being forgotten while still alive. הָיִיתִי כִּכְלִי אֹבֵד (hāyîtî kiklî ʾōbêd, "I have become like a shattered clay vessel") — kelî: vessel, instrument; ʾōbêd: perishing, destroyed. The broken pottery that has no further use and is discarded.
13: כִּי שָׁמַעְתִּי דִּבַּת רַבִּים (kî shāmaʿtî dibbat rabbîm, "I hear the rumors of the many") — dibbāh: a whispering, a defamatory report — the gossip-network. מָגוֹר מִסָּבִיב (māgôr missābîb, "terror surrounds me") — māgôr missābîb is a Jeremian phrase (Jeremiah 20:3, 10) — "terror on every side." Jeremiah uses it of himself and ironically gives it as the name of the priest who arrested him. Psalm and prophet share a vocabulary of total encirclement by threat.
14: וַאֲנִי עָלֶיךָ בָטַחְתִּי יְהוָה (waʾănî ʿālêkā bāṭaḥtî YHWH, "But I trusted in you, LORD") — the wĕʾănî (but I) counter to all of vv9-13: despite abandonment, wasting, forgotten status, and surrounding terror — the Psalmist's orientation remains. אָמַרְתִּי אֱלֹהַי אָתָּה (ʾāmartî ʾĕlōhay ʾāttāh, "I said, 'You are my God'") — the basic confession of covenant relationship: "my God." In the middle of catastrophe, the Psalmist speaks the relational claim.
15: בְּיָדְךָ עִתֹּתָי (bĕyādĕkā ʿittōtāy, "My times are in your hand") — ʿittîm: times, seasons, appointed moments (plural of ʿêt: a fixed time). Every moment of vulnerability and opportunity held in the divine hand. Not fate but covenantal custody: the same hand into which the spirit was committed (v5) now holds all the moments of the life. הַצִּילֵנִי מִיַּד-אֹיְבַי (haṣṣîlênî miyad-ʾōyĕbay, "rescue me from my enemies") — the contest of which hand holds the Psalmist: the enemy's hand or the LORD's.
16: הָאִירָה פָנֶיךָ עַל-עַבְדֶּךָ (hāʾîrāh pānêkā ʿal-ʿabdekā, "Make your face shine on your servant") — the priestly blessing of Numbers 6:25 ("the LORD make his face shine on you"): the illuminated divine face as the image of favorable presence. Contrast v7b: when the divine face was hidden, terror; when it shines, salvation. הוֹשִׁיעֵנִי בְחַסְדֶּךָ (hôshîʿênî bĕḥasdekā, "save me through your steadfast love") — grounded in ḥesed: rescue flows from covenant loyalty, not from the Psalmist's worthiness.
17: יֵבֹשׁוּ רְשָׁעִים יִדְּמוּ לִשְׁאוֹל (yêbōshû rĕshāʿîm yiddĕmû lishĕʾôl, "let the wicked be ashamed — let them go silently down to Sheol") — bûsh: to be ashamed, put to shame (the social disgrace of public failure). Dāmam: to be silent — the silence of Sheol (as opposed to the singing of the rescued Psalmist in Psalms 30:12). The reversal: the Psalmist, who was shamed and nearly silent, prays that the wicked get what they intended for him.
18: תֵּאָלַמְנָה שִׂפְתֵי-שָׁקֶר (tēʾālamĕnāh śiptê-shāqer, "Let the lying lips be struck silent") — ʾālam (niphal: to be silenced, made mute): the lips that spoke the dibbāh (defamatory whispers) of v13 silenced. הַדֹּבְרוֹת עַל-צַדִּיק עָתָק בְּגַאֲוָה וָבוּז — ʿātāq: insolent, arrogant things; gaʾăwāh: pride; bûz: contempt. The attitude behind the speech: contempt for the righteous.
19: מָה-רַב טוּבְךָ אֲשֶׁר-צָפַנְתָּ לִּירֵאֶיךָ (māh-rab ṭûbĕkā ʾăsher-ṣāpantā lîrêʾêkā, "How great is your goodness, which you have stored up for those who fear you") — ṭôb: goodness, good things. Ṣāpan: to store up, to hide away for safekeeping — the goodness of God held in reserve like treasure. Lîrêʾêkā: "for those who fear you" — yirʾāh (fear of God) as the qualifier of who receives it. פָּעַלְתָּ לַחֹסִים בָּךְ — ḥāsāh (participle: those who take refuge): the shelter-seekers for whom stored goodness is actualized.
20: תַּסְתִּירֵם בְּסֵתֶר פָּנֶיךָ מֵרֻכְסֵי אִישׁ (tastîrêm bĕsêter pānêkā mêrukhsê ʾîsh, "In the shelter of your presence you hide them from human plots") — divine hiddenness now used positively: the sêter pānim (shelter of the face) as protective concealment. The very face whose hiding brought terror (v7) becomes the shelter of those who trust. תִּצְפְּנֵם בְּסֻכָּה מֵרִיב לְשֹׁנוֹת — hidden in the divine sukkāh (tabernacle/booth) from the quarreling of tongues — connecting to the Festival of Booths and divine dwelling among Israel.
21: בָּרוּךְ יְהוָה כִּי-הִפְלִיא חַסְדּוֹ לִי (bārûk YHWH kî-hipllîʾ ḥasdô lî, "Praise the LORD, for he displayed his wonderful steadfast love to me") — pālāʾ (hiphil: to make wonderful, to act in a marvelous way): ḥesed niphlāʾ — love that operates beyond normal expectation. בְּעִיר מָצוֹר (bĕʿîr māṣôr, "when I was like a man in a besieged city") — māṣôr: siege — the city under siege as the situation of maximum danger. The Psalmist felt encircled (terror surrounds, v13) as a city under siege, yet from within that encirclement, divine love was displayed.
22: וַאֲנִי אָמַרְתִּי בְחָפְזִי (waʾănî ʾāmartî bĕḥāpĕzî, "I said in my panic") — ḥāpaz: to flee in haste, to be panicked — a significant word: it appears in Exodus 12:11 and Deuteronomy 16:3 for the hasty departure from Egypt (bĕḥippāzôn: "in haste"). The Psalmist's panic uses the exodus-departure word — his inner flight from composed trust. נִגְרַזְתִּי מִנֶּגֶד עֵינֶיךָ (nigrăztî minneged ʿênêkā, "I am cut off from your sight") — the feeling of severance from the divine gaze. שָׁמַעְתָּ קוֹל תַּחֲנוּנַי — the truth under the panic: the feeling was wrong; the hearing was real.
23: אֶהֱבוּ אֶת-יְהוָה כָּל-חֲסִידָיו (ʾehĕbû ʾet-YHWH kol-ḥăsîdāyw, "Love the LORD, all you his faithful people!") — the move from individual testimony to community summons: the Psalmist's experience becomes the ground of exhortation to all the ḥăsîdîm. יְהוָה שֹׁמֵר אֱמוּנִים (YHWH shōmêr ʾĕmûnîm, "The LORD protects the faithful") — ʾĕmûnîm: the faithful ones (from ʾāman: to be firm, reliable, faithful). God watches over (shāmar) those characterized by ʾemûnāh (faithfulness).
24: חִזְקוּ וְיַאֲמֵץ לְבַבְכֶם (ḥizqû wĕyaʾămêṣ lĕbabkem, "Be strong, and let your heart be courageous") — ḥāzaq (to be strong) and ʾāmaṣ (to be bold/courageous) — the same two verbs of the Deuteronomic commission: "Be strong and courageous" (ḥazaq weʾĕmāṣ, Deuteronomy 31:6-7; Joshua 1:6-9). כָּל-הַמְיַחֲלִים לַיהוָה (kol-hĕmĕyaḥălîm laYHWH, "all you who hope in the LORD!") — yāḥal: to wait with expectation, to hope actively. The Psalm ends with the summons to those who are in the position the Psalmist was in at the beginning — in distress, waiting for the LORD to act.
